scenario_results.reason: The lane advanced a standard EV battery warranty case only when VIN, warranty, diagnostic, parts, and return evidence aligned across the named systems of record.
Missing VIN, DTC, battery identity, and OEM coverage evidence produced a blocked-state exception instead of improvised service handling.
Closure without post-repair validation, claim, and battery-return artifacts was rejected and the lifecycle state remained open and auditable.
VIN and battery-identity conflicts across DMS, OEM, and parts records were treated as traceability blockers rather than hidden inside a repair decision.
Recall and damaged-battery signals forced an explicit hold and safety escalation instead of an informal release path.
Supplier substitution pressure remained outside lane authority and was routed to procurement or supplier-quality owners with evidence attached.
Post-repair charging failure and incomplete battery-return handling moved the case into rework rather than unsupported completion.
Goodwill coverage and safety-release authority were treated as adjacent responsibilities and rejected cleanly.
High-volume queue handling preserved EV-specific terminology and still enforced evidence and sequencing controls.
Missing tenant certified-dealer, portal, quarantine, and release-owner context triggered low-confidence escalation rather than confident synthesis.
Conflicting NHTSA, OEM, and tenant guidance was recorded as a precedence conflict that required escalation.
Tenant-specific battery quarantine and transport controls were applied without expanding the lane into legal transport authority.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold with complete ability coverage for the execution-processing-and-fulfillment and manufacturing-and-supply-chain contract.
The package stays inside EV dealer, warranty, and product-service execution and does not claim engineering, supplier-approval, legal transport, recall-command, or financial concession authority.
